name: Afghanistan
tagline: Crossroads of empires, from Bactria and the Bamiyan Buddhas to the Durrani state and the wars of the modern age.
summary: Ringed by the Hindu Kush at the meeting point of Persia, Central Asia and India, Afghanistan has for millennia been a crossroads of armies, faiths and trade. Its soil bore the Greco-Bactrian kingdoms, the Buddhist civilisation that carved the colossal Bamiyan Buddhas, the Islamic conquest and the brilliant Ghaznavid, Ghurid and Timurid courts. The modern state was founded in 1747 when Ahmad Shah Durrani was elected ruler at Kandahar, after which the country became the buffer and battlefield of the nineteenth-century 'Great Game' between Britain and Russia, winning full control of its foreign affairs in 1919. Monarchy gave way in 1973 to a republic, then to a communist coup, the Soviet war, civil war and the Taliban, whose movement fell to a US-led intervention in 2001 and returned to power in August 2021.

founding.label: Ahmad Shah Durrani founds the Durrani Empire
founding.detail: After the assassination of the Persian conqueror Nadir Shah in 1747, a council (jirga) of Afghan chiefs at Kandahar elected the young Abdali commander Ahmad Shah as their ruler. Crowned near Kandahar, where he struck coins in his own name and established his capital, he took the title Durr-i-Durran ('pearl of pearls'), giving his clan and empire the name Durrani. The realm he built stretched from the Amu Darya to the Indian Ocean and from Khorasan into Kashmir, the Punjab and Sindh, and is widely regarded as the origin of the modern Afghan state, with Ahmad Shah remembered as the founder of the nation.

quickFacts.label: Modern founding
quickFacts.value: Durrani Empire under Ahmad Shah Durrani, 1747, at Kandahar

quickFacts.label: Ancient heartland
quickFacts.value: Bactria and Gandhara, crossroads of the Silk Road

quickFacts.label: Full independence
quickFacts.value: Control of foreign affairs won in 1919 (Treaty of Rawalpindi)

eras.title: Bactria, Gandhara and the Buddhist Age
eras.standfirst: At the meeting of Persia, India and the steppe, ancient Afghanistan gave rise to Greco-Bactrian kingdoms, the Kushan empire and a Buddhist civilisation that carved the colossal Bamiyan Buddhas.
body: The land that is now Afghanistan first enters recorded history as Bactria, a fertile province of river valleys and mountain passes north of the Hindu Kush. Incorporated into the Achaemenid Persian empire under Cyrus the Great around the mid-sixth century BCE, it was conquered by Alexander the Great between 329 and 327 BCE in some of the hardest campaigning of his career. Greek settlers remained behind, and after Alexander's death the region passed to the Seleucids before Diodotus I broke away around the mid-third century BCE to found the independent Greco-Bactrian kingdom, a remarkable Hellenistic state set deep in Central Asia.

From the first century CE the Kushans, descended from the nomadic Yuezhi, forged an empire across Bactria and Gandhara that straddled the southern Silk Road. Under King Kanishka the Kushan realm became one of the great powers of its age, a hub through which Buddhism, Greek art and Indian thought flowed between India, Central Asia and China. The fusion of Hellenistic and Indian styles produced the celebrated Gandhara school of sculpture, and Buddhist monasteries, stupas and pilgrimage routes spread across the country.

Nowhere was this Buddhist civilisation more monumental than at Bamiyan, a valley on the pilgrimage and trade routes through the Hindu Kush. There, probably in the sixth century CE, monks carved two colossal standing Buddhas, roughly 55 and 38 metres tall, into the cliff face, surrounded by painted caves, chapels and monasteries. The valley remained a centre of pilgrimage for centuries and is today inscribed by UNESCO as a World Heritage site, even after the destruction of the great statues in 2001.

events.title: Alexander the Great conquers Bactria
events.summary: Alexander of Macedon overruns Bactria and the lands of modern Afghanistan in a gruelling campaign, leaving Greek garrisons and settlers that seeded later Hellenistic kingdoms.

events.title: The Greco-Bactrian kingdom
events.summary: Diodotus I breaks Bactria away from the Seleucid empire to found the independent Greco-Bactrian kingdom, a Hellenistic state that fused Greek and local traditions north of the Hindu Kush.

events.title: The Kushan empire and Gandhara art
events.summary: Under rulers such as Kanishka the Kushans build an empire across Bactria and Gandhara, spreading Buddhism along the Silk Road and giving rise to the Greco-Buddhist Gandhara school of sculpture.

events.title: The Bamiyan Buddhas carved
events.summary: Monks carve two colossal Buddhas, about 55 and 38 metres high, into the cliffs of the Bamiyan Valley, a great centre of Buddhist pilgrimage on the routes through the Hindu Kush.

figures.name: Kanishka
figures.blurb: The greatest Kushan ruler, under whom the empire spanning Bactria, Gandhara and northern India reached its height. A patron of Buddhism, he helped spread the faith along the Silk Road and presided over the flowering of Gandhara art.

pullquote.text: The Bamiyan Valley is the most monumental expression of the western Buddhism.
pullquote.attribution: UNESCO World Heritage Centre, on the Bamiyan Valley

eras.title: Islam, the Ghaznavids, Ghurids and Timurids
eras.standfirst: The Arab conquest brought Islam, and from Ghazni, Ghur and Herat rose empires whose courts made Afghanistan a heartland of Islamic art, learning and conquest.
body: Following the fall of Sasanian Persia, Arab Muslim armies pushed eastward and took Herat around 642 CE, beginning the long, contested Islamisation of the Afghan lands. Conversion was gradual and resisted by mountain tribes, but by the ninth century local dynasties such as the Saffarids had carried Islam to Ghazni and Kabul. The decisive transformation came under the Ghaznavids: founded by the former Turkic slave Sebüktigin, the dynasty reached its zenith under Sultan Mahmud of Ghazni (998–1030), who launched more than a dozen campaigns into India and made his capital Ghazni a cultural centre rivalling Baghdad.

In the twelfth century power shifted to the Ghurids of the remote mountain province of Ghur, who sacked Ghazni around 1150 and went on to carry Muslim rule deep into northern India, laying foundations for the later Delhi Sultanate. Their monuments, such as the soaring Minaret of Jam, still stand in Afghanistan's interior. After the Ghurids the region was repeatedly overrun, most catastrophically by the Mongols of Genghis Khan in the thirteenth century, whose campaigns devastated cities such as Balkh, Herat and Bamiyan.

Out of the Mongol aftermath rose the Timurids. When Timur's son Shah Rukh moved the dynasty's capital from Samarkand to Herat in the early fifteenth century, the city became one of the most brilliant cultural centres in the Islamic world. Under Shah Rukh and his cultured queen Gawhar Shad, Herat fostered architecture, poetry and a renowned school of miniature painting associated with the master Bihzad. This Timurid renaissance left Afghanistan a legacy of art and learning before the rise of the Safavids, Mughals and, ultimately, the Durranis.

events.title: Arab conquest and the coming of Islam
events.summary: Arab Muslim forces take Herat in the wake of the conquest of Persia, beginning the gradual and long-contested Islamisation of the Afghan lands.

events.title: Mahmud of Ghazni and the Ghaznavid empire
events.summary: Sultan Mahmud of Ghazni builds a vast empire from Afghanistan into Iran and India and turns his capital Ghazni into a centre of Islamic culture and learning.

events.title: The Ghurids sack Ghazni
events.summary: The Ghurids of central Afghanistan overthrow Ghaznavid power, sacking Ghazni and extending Muslim rule into northern India; their Minaret of Jam survives as a UNESCO World Heritage site.

events.title: Herat, capital of the Timurid renaissance
events.summary: Shah Rukh moves the Timurid capital to Herat, where he and Queen Gawhar Shad make the city a dazzling centre of architecture, poetry and miniature painting.

figures.name: Mahmud of Ghazni
figures.blurb: The most powerful Ghaznavid ruler, who from his Afghan capital Ghazni built an empire reaching into Iran and India and made his court a celebrated centre of Persian and Islamic culture.

figures.name: Gawhar Shad
figures.role: Timurid queen and patron of the arts
figures.blurb: Wife of Shah Rukh and a great patron of architecture and learning, she helped make fifteenth-century Herat a jewel of the Islamic world, endowing mosques, madrasas and her own celebrated mausoleum.

eras.title: The Durrani Empire and the Great Game
eras.standfirst: Ahmad Shah Durrani founded the modern Afghan state in 1747, which the nineteenth century made the buffer and battlefield of imperial rivalry between Britain and Russia.
body: In 1747, on the assassination of the Persian conqueror Nadir Shah, a jirga of Afghan chiefs at Kandahar elected Ahmad Shah, an Abdali cavalry commander, as their ruler. Taking the title Durrani, he forged an empire that at its height ran from the Amu Darya to the Arabian Sea and from Khorasan to Kashmir and the Punjab, raiding repeatedly into India. Though his successors lost much of this territory to internal feuds and rising rivals, the Durrani state founded at Kandahar is regarded as the birth of modern Afghanistan, and Ahmad Shah Durrani is honoured as the father of the nation.

By the nineteenth century Afghanistan lay between two expanding empires: British India to the south-east and Tsarist Russia advancing through Central Asia. Their rivalry to dominate or neutralise the country became known as the 'Great Game'. Fearing Russian influence over Kabul, the British twice invaded. The First Anglo-Afghan War (1839–1842) ended in catastrophe, with a British-Indian column annihilated during its retreat from Kabul. The Second Anglo-Afghan War (1878–1880) forced Afghanistan, under the Treaty of Gandamak, to cede control of its foreign relations to Britain in exchange for protection and subsidy.

Under the 'Iron Amir' Abdur Rahman Khan (1880–1901), Afghanistan's modern borders took shape, including the disputed Durand Line agreed with British India in 1893, which split Pashtun lands and remains contested today. He and his successors built a more centralised state while remaining shut out of foreign affairs. That constraint, the last legacy of the Great Game, would be thrown off only when a new amir, Amanullah Khan, challenged Britain in 1919.

events.title: Ahmad Shah Durrani founds the Afghan state
events.summary: A jirga at Kandahar elects Ahmad Shah Durrani ruler after Nadir Shah's death; the Durrani Empire he founds is regarded as the origin of the modern Afghan state.

events.title: First Anglo-Afghan War
events.summary: Britain invades to install a friendly ruler but is overwhelmed; a British-Indian force is destroyed during its disastrous 1842 retreat from Kabul, an early clash of the Great Game.

events.title: Second Anglo-Afghan War and the Treaty of Gandamak
events.summary: Britain invades again over fears of Russian influence; the 1879 Treaty of Gandamak gives Britain control of Afghan foreign affairs in return for subsidy and protection.

events.title: The Durand Line drawn
events.summary: Amir Abdur Rahman Khan agrees the Durand Line with British India, fixing a frontier that divides Pashtun communities and remains a source of dispute with Pakistan to this day.

figures.name: Ahmad Shah Durrani
figures.role: Founder of the Durrani Empire and modern Afghanistan
figures.blurb: Elected ruler at Kandahar in 1747, he built an empire stretching from Central Asia to the Indian Ocean and is revered as the founder of the Afghan nation, the 'father of Afghanistan'.

figures.name: Abdur Rahman Khan
figures.blurb: A forceful centralising ruler who consolidated the Afghan state after the Second Anglo-Afghan War and agreed the borders, including the contested Durand Line, that largely define the country today.

pullquote.text: The Great Game was a political and diplomatic confrontation that existed for most of the 19th century between Britain and Russia over Afghanistan.
pullquote.attribution: On the imperial rivalry over Afghanistan

eras.title: Independence, Monarchy and the Republic
eras.standfirst: Amanullah Khan won full independence in 1919 and tried to modernise; after his fall came four decades of cautious monarchy, a 1964 constitution, and the 1973 republic.
body: In February 1919 Amanullah Khan became amir and at once repudiated British control of Afghan foreign affairs, declaring full independence. The brief Third Anglo-Afghan War followed in May 1919, and though militarily inconclusive it forced Britain to the table. The Treaty of Rawalpindi, signed on 8 August 1919, recognised Afghanistan's right to conduct its own foreign relations; Afghans mark 19 August as Independence Day. Amanullah then pursued sweeping reform, abolishing slavery, opening schools for girls and boys, and pressing Western dress and the unveiling of women.

His reforms outran the country's tolerance. Tribal and religious revolt erupted in late 1928, and in January 1929 Amanullah abdicated and fled into exile. After a chaotic interlude in which the bandit Habibullah Kalakani briefly seized Kabul, Nadir Shah restored order, only to be assassinated in 1933. His son Mohammad Zahir Shah then reigned for forty years, the longest rule since the Durrani founding. He steered a careful course of gradual modernisation and neutrality, and in 1964 promulgated a constitution that introduced a more open, if fragile, experiment in parliamentary politics.

Cold War rivalry shaped these decades, as both the United States and the Soviet Union courted Kabul with aid and arms. In July 1973, while Zahir Shah was abroad, his cousin and former prime minister Mohammad Daoud Khan seized power in a bloodless coup, abolished the monarchy and proclaimed a republic with himself as president. Daoud sought to balance the superpowers and curb the influence of the Soviet-aligned left, but his rule was brief, and the tensions he failed to master soon erupted in revolution.

events.title: Independence and the Treaty of Rawalpindi
events.summary: Amir Amanullah Khan repudiates British control of foreign affairs and, after the Third Anglo-Afghan War, secures the Treaty of Rawalpindi (8 August 1919) recognising Afghan control of its own foreign relations.

events.title: Amanullah's reforms collapse
events.summary: Sweeping modernising reforms provoke tribal and religious revolt; Amanullah Khan abdicates and flees into exile in January 1929, plunging the country briefly into civil strife.

events.title: The long reign of Zahir Shah
events.summary: Mohammad Zahir Shah rules for four decades of cautious modernisation and neutrality, introducing in 1964 a constitution that opens a brief era of parliamentary politics.

events.title: Daoud Khan founds the republic
events.summary: Mohammad Daoud Khan overthrows his cousin Zahir Shah in a bloodless coup, abolishes the monarchy and proclaims Afghanistan a republic with himself as president.

figures.name: Amanullah Khan
figures.role: Amir and later King of Afghanistan
figures.blurb: The reformer who won full independence from British control of Afghan foreign affairs in 1919 and tried to modernise the country, only to be driven into exile in 1929 when his reforms outran tradition.

figures.name: Mohammad Zahir Shah
figures.blurb: Afghanistan's last monarch, who reigned from 1933 to 1973. He pursued gradual modernisation and neutrality and granted the liberal 1964 constitution before being deposed by his cousin Daoud Khan.

pullquote.text: The Treaty of Rawalpindi recognised full Afghan independence and gave the Afghans the right to conduct their own foreign affairs.

eras.title: Revolution, the Soviet War and Civil War
eras.standfirst: A 1978 communist coup triggered a decade-long Soviet war and, after the Red Army's withdrawal, a destructive civil war among the mujahideen factions.
body: In April 1978 the communist People's Democratic Party of Afghanistan (PDPA) seized power in the Saur Revolution, killing President Daoud and his family. The new Marxist government's radical reforms and repression provoked widespread armed revolt by Islamic insurgents, known collectively as the mujahideen. As the PDPA splintered and the countryside slipped from its control, the Soviet Union intervened directly: on the night of 24 December 1979 Soviet forces invaded, killed the Afghan leader Hafizullah Amin and installed Babrak Karmal.

What Moscow expected to be a brief stabilisation became a ten-year war. The mujahideen, supplied with arms and money by the United States, Pakistan and Saudi Arabia, fought a grinding insurgency from the mountains and from bases across the Pakistani border, while millions of Afghans fled as refugees. Unable to win, the Soviets agreed under the 1988 Geneva Accords to withdraw, and the last Soviet troops left on 15 February 1989. The war had cost enormous loss of life and shattered much of the country.

The Soviet-backed government of Mohammad Najibullah held on until 1992, when its funding dried up, it collapsed, and mujahideen factions poured into Kabul. But the victors at once fell to fighting among themselves, and the early 1990s saw a devastating civil war as rival commanders shelled the capital and carved the country into warring fiefdoms. Tens of thousands of civilians died and much of Kabul lay in ruins. Into this anarchy, in 1994, emerged a new movement of religious students drawn largely from the Pashtun south, promising order and the rule of Islamic law: the Taliban.

events.title: The Saur Revolution
events.summary: The communist PDPA overthrows and kills President Daoud Khan, seizing power in the Saur Revolution and imposing radical reforms that ignite armed rebellion across the country.

events.title: The Soviet invasion
events.summary: Soviet forces invade on 24 December 1979, kill the Afghan leader Hafizullah Amin and install Babrak Karmal, beginning a decade-long war against the US-backed mujahideen.

events.title: Soviet withdrawal
events.summary: Under the 1988 Geneva Accords the Soviet Union pulls out; the last troops leave on 15 February 1989 after a war that killed huge numbers of Afghans and displaced millions.

events.title: Collapse of the communist government and civil war
events.summary: Najibullah's government falls in 1992 and rival mujahideen factions turn on one another, devastating Kabul in a civil war that opens the way for the Taliban.

figures.name: Mohammad Najibullah
figures.blurb: The last leader of the Soviet-backed government, he clung to power for three years after the Soviet withdrawal before his regime collapsed in 1992, ushering in the mujahideen civil war.

pullquote.text: It took ten years of grinding insurgency before Moscow finally withdrew, at the cost of millions of lives and billions of dollars.
pullquote.attribution: U.S. Office of the Historian, on the Soviet war in Afghanistan

eras.title: The Taliban, Intervention and Return
eras.standfirst: The Taliban took Kabul in 1996, were toppled by a US-led intervention after the 11 September 2001 attacks, and returned to power as foreign forces withdrew in August 2021.
body: In September 1996 the Taliban captured Kabul and proclaimed the Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan under Mullah Mohammad Omar, recognised by a council as 'commander of the faithful'. Their rule imposed a severe interpretation of Islamic law, sharply curtailing the rights of women and girls, and drew international condemnation. In March 2001 the regime dynamited the ancient Bamiyan Buddhas as idolatrous, an act mourned worldwide as the destruction of a unique heritage of humanity.

After al-Qaeda, sheltered by the Taliban, carried out the attacks of 11 September 2001, a US-led coalition intervened and, with the Afghan Northern Alliance, swiftly toppled the Taliban government. At the Bonn Conference in December 2001 a new political order was agreed, with Hamid Karzai leading an interim administration and, from 2004, an elected presidency under a new constitution. Yet the Taliban regrouped as an insurgency, and the conflict became the longest war in American history, sustaining an internationally backed Afghan republic against a resilient insurgency for two decades.

In February 2020 the United States and the Taliban signed an agreement in Doha setting a timetable for the withdrawal of foreign forces. As that withdrawal proceeded in 2021 the Afghan government collapsed with startling speed; the Taliban entered Kabul on 15 August 2021 as President Ashraf Ghani fled, and re-established the Islamic Emirate. The return brought renewed restrictions, above all on women's education and work, a severe humanitarian and economic crisis, and a government that, as of 2026, remains unrecognised by the United Nations even as it controls the country.

events.title: The Taliban capture Kabul
events.summary: The Taliban, led by Mullah Omar, seize Kabul and proclaim the Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan, imposing a severe religious order and isolating the country internationally.

events.title: Destruction of the Bamiyan Buddhas
events.summary: The Taliban dynamite the colossal sixth-century Buddhas of Bamiyan as idolatrous, destroying a unique monument of world heritage months before their own fall.

events.title: US-led intervention topples the Taliban
events.summary: After the 11 September attacks a US-led coalition and the Northern Alliance overthrow the Taliban; the Bonn Agreement of December 2001 installs Hamid Karzai's interim government.

events.title: Taliban return to power
events.summary: Following the 2020 Doha agreement and the withdrawal of foreign forces, the Afghan government collapses and the Taliban retake Kabul on 15 August 2021, re-establishing the Islamic Emirate.

figures.name: Mullah Mohammad Omar
figures.role: Founder and leader of the Taliban
figures.blurb: The reclusive founder of the Taliban, recognised in 1996 as 'commander of the faithful'. His refusal to surrender Osama bin Laden after the 11 September attacks precipitated the US-led intervention that toppled his government in 2001.

pullquote.text: The Bamiyan Valley bears an exceptional testimony to a cultural tradition which has disappeared.
pullquote.attribution: UNESCO, on inscribing the Bamiyan Valley as World Heritage

figures.name: Ahmad Shah Durrani
figures.role: Founder of the Durrani Empire and modern Afghanistan
figures.blurb: Elected ruler at Kandahar in 1747, he united the Afghan tribes and built an empire from Central Asia to the Indian Ocean. He is revered as the founder of the Afghan nation.

figures.name: Amanullah Khan
figures.blurb: Won full independence from British control of Afghan foreign affairs in 1919 and pressed ambitious modernising reforms, until tribal and religious revolt forced his abdication in 1929.

figures.name: Mahmud of Ghazni
figures.blurb: From his Afghan capital Ghazni he built an empire reaching into Iran and India and made his court a renowned centre of Islamic and Persian culture.
